title: Mumps
oneLiner: Acute viral paramyxovirus infection causing parotid swelling; most cases self-limiting but can cause serious extra-salivary complications (meningitis, orchitis, pancreatitis).

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Meningoencephalitis** – altered GCS, seizures, focal neurology, photophobia. *Single finding: neck stiffness + Glasgow ≤14 in a patient with parotitis → do not just treat “viral meningitis”; urgent CT + LP required.*
- **Testicular torsion** – acute unilateral scrotal pain with nausea/vomiting and absent cremasteric reflex. *Single finding: high-riding testis + absent blood flow on Doppler (not just tenderness). Do not attribute to mumps orchitis until torsion excluded.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Prodrome (12–48 h): low-grade fever, malaise, myalgia, headache.
  - Unilateral then bilateral parotid swelling (tender, non-erythematous, obscures angle of mandible).
  - Otalgia, trismus, pain on chewing sour/spicy foods.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Swelling over parotid gland (ear lobe lifted, angle of mandible obscured).
  - Stensen’s duct orifice may be red and oedematous – no purulent discharge (if purulent → bacterial parotitis).
  - Check testicular exam in males (tenderness, swelling – usually 7–10 days after parotitis).
  - Neurological exam: meningism, cranial nerve signs (especially VII – facial nerve palsy rare but reported).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Bacterial parotitis** – unilateral, purulent discharge from Stensen’s duct, high fever, neutrophilia. *Splitter: purulent duct discharge + toxic appearance → bacterial, not mumps.*
  - **Other viral infections** – EBV (splenomegaly, lymphocytosis), CMV, HIV (persistent parotid swelling).
  - **Sialolithiasis / salivary gland tumour** – gradual onset, intermittent, no prodrome, no fever.
  - **Cervical lymphadenopathy** – nodes are discrete, not obscuring mandible, usually not bilateral.
- **Splitter:** “Swelling obscuring the angle of mandible + bilateral → mumps.” Unilateral swelling with duct pus → bacterial.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Check oral cavity: Stensen’s duct appearance, express for pus.
  - Testicular exam: if pain → urgent scrotal Doppler ultrasound.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (lymphocytosis typical; neutrophilia suggests bacterial).
  - CRP/ESR (mildly elevated; very high suggests bacterial).
  - Serum amylase (often elevated in parotitis; also check if abdominal pain – rule out pancreatitis).
  - Mumps serology: IgM (acute) and IgG (convalescent – rarely needed acutely).
  - Consider EBV/CMV serology if atypical.
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: None routinely needed.
  - Second-line: Ultrasound of parotid if suspected abscess or atypical (bilateral assess).
  - If meningism: CT head (to rule out raised ICP) then LP.
- **Special tests:**
  - Lumbar puncture if meningism: CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, raised protein.
  - Viral PCR (CSF/throat swab/urine) via reference lab.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Clinical diagnosis (parotitis + fever + exposure) is usually sufficient. Confirmatory serology or PCR for public health notification, but management is same.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age (mumps in first trimester may increase miscarriage risk; also guides imaging if needed).
  - **Scrotal Doppler** if testicular pain (to exclude torsion – irreversible within hours).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Airway assessment: rare but massive parotitis can cause airway compromise – call anaesthetics if stridor.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1 g PO/IV qds (if weight >50 kg) ± ibuprofen 400 mg PO tds.
  - Hydration: encourage oral fluids; IV if vomiting or unable to swallow.
  - Isolation: place in single room with airborne/droplet precautions for 5 days after onset of parotitis.
  - Notify local Public Health/Health Protection Team (notifiable disease in UK/IE).
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Supportive care: rest, soft diet, avoid acidic juices (increase pain).
  - Monitor for complications: daily NEWS, GCS, testicular exam, abdominal pain.
  - If orchitis: scrotal support (e.g., tight underwear), NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO tds), consider urology referral if severe pain or suspected torsion excluded.
  - If meningitis: treat as viral – no antivirals; manage raised ICP if needed (neurosurgery consult).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Usually discharged once afebrile, eating/drinking, no complications.
  - Avoid school/work until 5 days after parotitis onset (or until swelling subsides if no swab confirmation).
  - Offer MMR vaccine to exposed contacts if not vaccinated (within 72 h of exposure may reduce risk).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s at maintenance rate if unable to drink.
  - VTE prophylaxis: not routinely needed unless immobile or other risk factors – use LMWH per local policy.
  - Diet: soft/light foods, encourage oral fluids; NBM only if severe trismus or airway concern.
  - Observation: NEWS 4-hourly; if meningitis features increase to 1–2 hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Paracetamol (oral/IV): 1 g qds (max 4 g/day) for pain/fever.
  - Ibuprofen (oral): 400 mg tds (max 1.2 g/day) for inflammation, especially orchitis.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Codeine (30–60 mg PO qds) if pain not controlled – but use with caution, avoid in children.
  - Corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone) for severe orchitis with unrelenting pain – dose: [check local policy] (typical short course 0.5–1 mg/kg/day for 5–7 days, but evidence limited).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid aspirin in children (<16 years) due to Reye’s syndrome risk.
  - NSAIDs: avoid in aspirin-sensitive asthma, peptic ulcer disease, renal impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Paracetamol: reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (max 2 g/day).
  - Ibuprofen: avoid if eGFR <30 mL/min.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Paracetamol overdose** – accidental stacking of IV and oral routes (max 1 g qds total). Double-check prescription. Never exceed 4 g/24 h.
  - **Ibuprofen in dehydrated young men with orchitis** – can precipitate acute kidney injury. Ensure adequate hydration first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Orchitis** (~15–30% of post-pubertal males): usually unilateral, painful, rarely leads to atrophy. Bilateral orchitis can cause subfertility.
  - **Meningitis** (~10%): lymphocytic meningitis – usually benign, full recovery.
  - **Encephalitis** (~1%): more severe – seizures, altered consciousness, focal signs.
  - **Pancreatitis** (rare): epigastric pain, vomiting, raised amylase/lipase.
  - **Sensorineural hearing loss** (rare, usually permanent – usually unilateral).
- **Chronic:**
  - Subfertility (rare after bilateral orchitis).
  - Persistent neurological deficits (very rare after encephalitis).
sections.Prognosis: - >95% of cases fully recover within 1–2 weeks. Mortality <0.1% (mostly from encephalitis). Orchitis resolves over 1–2 weeks; risk of testicular atrophy ~30% if orchitis occurs. Hearing loss is permanent in up to 1 in 20,000 cases.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask vaccination history** – most cases occur in unvaccinated adolescents/young adults. Check MMR records.
- **Notify Public Health immediately** – they will arrange contact tracing and post-exposure prophylaxis for vulnerable groups (e.g., pregnant women, immunosuppressed).
- **Perform a testicular examination in every male >10 years with mumps** – even if no pain initially. Document and plan scrotal Doppler if tenderness.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe antibiotics** – mumps is viral. Widespread use contributes to resistance and side-effects.
- **Do not discharge a testicular pain without documenting cremasteric reflex and arranging urgent Doppler if suspicious** – miss torsion = loss of testis.
- **Do not give corticosteroids for routine parotitis** – reserved only for severe orchitis under specialist advice. May risk immunosuppression if meningitis present.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, or confusion → meningitis.
  - New severe testicular pain (especially if unilateral) – return immediately for torsion assessment.
  - New epigastric pain with vomiting → pancreatitis.
  - Hearing loss, tinnitus, or vertigo – urgent audiology referral.
  - Fever >39°C persisting >48 h – consider bacterial superinfection.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Routine: no specific follow-up unless complications occur. Advise GP if symptoms not improving by 1 week.
  - Audiological follow-up if any hearing symptoms.
  - Fertility counselling if bilateral orchitis with persistent testicular atrophy: refer to andrology clinic.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** (especially with neurological deterioration).
  - **GCS <15** or any new focal neurology → ?encephalitis; need urgent CT + LP.
  - **Seizures** → treat as status; call senior anaesthetist/neurologist.
  - **Severe scrotal pain with absent cremasteric reflex** → urgent urology referral and Doppler ultrasound (within 1 hour).
  - **Airway compromise** (stridor, drooling, inability to swallow secretions) → call anaesthetics, prepare for difficult airway.
  - **Suspected intracranial hypertension** (papilloedema, Cushing’s reflex) → senior review, consider CT and neurosurgery referral.

rawMarkdown:
## Mumps
**One-liner:** Acute viral paramyxovirus infection causing parotid swelling; most cases self-limiting but can cause serious extra-salivary complications (meningitis, orchitis, pancreatitis).

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Meningoencephalitis** – altered GCS, seizures, focal neurology, photophobia. *Single finding: neck stiffness + Glasgow ≤14 in a patient with parotitis → do not just treat “viral meningitis”; urgent CT + LP required.*
- **Testicular torsion** – acute unilateral scrotal pain with nausea/vomiting and absent cremasteric reflex. *Single finding: high-riding testis + absent blood flow on Doppler (not just tenderness). Do not attribute to mumps orchitis until torsion excluded.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Prodrome (12–48 h): low-grade fever, malaise, myalgia, headache.
  - Unilateral then bilateral parotid swelling (tender, non-erythematous, obscures angle of mandible).
  - Otalgia, trismus, pain on chewing sour/spicy foods.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Swelling over parotid gland (ear lobe lifted, angle of mandible obscured).
  - Stensen’s duct orifice may be red and oedematous – no purulent discharge (if purulent → bacterial parotitis).
  - Check testicular exam in males (tenderness, swelling – usually 7–10 days after parotitis).
  - Neurological exam: meningism, cranial nerve signs (especially VII – facial nerve palsy rare but reported).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Bacterial parotitis** – unilateral, purulent discharge from Stensen’s duct, high fever, neutrophilia. *Splitter: purulent duct discharge + toxic appearance → bacterial, not mumps.*
  - **Other viral infections** – EBV (splenomegaly, lymphocytosis), CMV, HIV (persistent parotid swelling).
  - **Sialolithiasis / salivary gland tumour** – gradual onset, intermittent, no prodrome, no fever.
  - **Cervical lymphadenopathy** – nodes are discrete, not obscuring mandible, usually not bilateral.
- **Splitter:** “Swelling obscuring the angle of mandible + bilateral → mumps.” Unilateral swelling with duct pus → bacterial.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Check oral cavity: Stensen’s duct appearance, express for pus.
  - Testicular exam: if pain → urgent scrotal Doppler ultrasound.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (lymphocytosis typical; neutrophilia suggests bacterial).
  - CRP/ESR (mildly elevated; very high suggests bacterial).
  - Serum amylase (often elevated in parotitis; also check if abdominal pain – rule out pancreatitis).
  - Mumps serology: IgM (acute) and IgG (convalescent – rarely needed acutely).
  - Consider EBV/CMV serology if atypical.
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: None routinely needed.
  - Second-line: Ultrasound of parotid if suspected abscess or atypical (bilateral assess).
  - If meningism: CT head (to rule out raised ICP) then LP.
- **Special tests:**
  - Lumbar puncture if meningism: CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, raised protein.
  - Viral PCR (CSF/throat swab/urine) via reference lab.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Clinical diagnosis (parotitis + fever + exposure) is usually sufficient. Confirmatory serology or PCR for public health notification, but management is same.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age (mumps in first trimester may increase miscarriage risk; also guides imaging if needed).
  - **Scrotal Doppler** if testicular pain (to exclude torsion – irreversible within hours).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Airway assessment: rare but massive parotitis can cause airway compromise – call anaesthetics if stridor.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1 g PO/IV qds (if weight >50 kg) ± ibuprofen 400 mg PO tds.
  - Hydration: encourage oral fluids; IV if vomiting or unable to swallow.
  - Isolation: place in single room with airborne/droplet precautions for 5 days after onset of parotitis.
  - Notify local Public Health/Health Protection Team (notifiable disease in UK/IE).
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Supportive care: rest, soft diet, avoid acidic juices (increase pain).
  - Monitor for complications: daily NEWS, GCS, testicular exam, abdominal pain.
  - If orchitis: scrotal support (e.g., tight underwear), NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO tds), consider urology referral if severe pain or suspected torsion excluded.
  - If meningitis: treat as viral – no antivirals; manage raised ICP if needed (neurosurgery consult).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Usually discharged once afebrile, eating/drinking, no complications.
  - Avoid school/work until 5 days after parotitis onset (or until swelling subsides if no swab confirmation).
  - Offer MMR vaccine to exposed contacts if not vaccinated (within 72 h of exposure may reduce risk).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s at maintenance rate if unable to drink.
  - VTE prophylaxis: not routinely needed unless immobile or other risk factors – use LMWH per local policy.
  - Diet: soft/light foods, encourage oral fluids; NBM only if severe trismus or airway concern.
  - Observation: NEWS 4-hourly; if meningitis features increase to 1–2 hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Paracetamol (oral/IV): 1 g qds (max 4 g/day) for pain/fever.
  - Ibuprofen (oral): 400 mg tds (max 1.2 g/day) for inflammation, especially orchitis.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Codeine (30–60 mg PO qds) if pain not controlled – but use with caution, avoid in children.
  - Corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone) for severe orchitis with unrelenting pain – dose: [check local policy] (typical short course 0.5–1 mg/kg/day for 5–7 days, but evidence limited).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid aspirin in children (<16 years) due to Reye’s syndrome risk.
  - NSAIDs: avoid in aspirin-sensitive asthma, peptic ulcer disease, renal impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Paracetamol: reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (max 2 g/day).
  - Ibuprofen: avoid if eGFR <30 mL/min.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Paracetamol overdose** – accidental stacking of IV and oral routes (max 1 g qds total). Double-check prescription. Never exceed 4 g/24 h.
  - **Ibuprofen in dehydrated young men with orchitis** – can precipitate acute kidney injury. Ensure adequate hydration first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Orchitis** (~15–30% of post-pubertal males): usually unilateral, painful, rarely leads to atrophy. Bilateral orchitis can cause subfertility.
  - **Meningitis** (~10%): lymphocytic meningitis – usually benign, full recovery.
  - **Encephalitis** (~1%): more severe – seizures, altered consciousness, focal signs.
  - **Pancreatitis** (rare): epigastric pain, vomiting, raised amylase/lipase.
  - **Sensorineural hearing loss** (rare, usually permanent – usually unilateral).
- **Chronic:**
  - Subfertility (rare after bilateral orchitis).
  - Persistent neurological deficits (very rare after encephalitis).

### Prognosis
- >95% of cases fully recover within 1–2 weeks. Mortality <0.1% (mostly from encephalitis). Orchitis resolves over 1–2 weeks; risk of testicular atrophy ~30% if orchitis occurs. Hearing loss is permanent in up to 1 in 20,000 cases.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always ask vaccination history** – most cases occur in unvaccinated adolescents/young adults. Check MMR records.
- **Notify Public Health immediately** – they will arrange contact tracing and post-exposure prophylaxis for vulnerable groups (e.g., pregnant women, immunosuppressed).
- **Perform a testicular examination in every male >10 years with mumps** – even if no pain initially. Document and plan scrotal Doppler if tenderness.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not prescribe antibiotics** – mumps is viral. Widespread use contributes to resistance and side-effects.
- **Do not discharge a testicular pain without documenting cremasteric reflex and arranging urgent Doppler if suspicious** – miss torsion = loss of testis.
- **Do not give corticosteroids for routine parotitis** – reserved only for severe orchitis under specialist advice. May risk immunosuppression if meningitis present.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, or confusion → meningitis.
  - New severe testicular pain (especially if unilateral) – return immediately for torsion assessment.
  - New epigastric pain with vomiting → pancreatitis.
  - Hearing loss, tinnitus, or vertigo – urgent audiology referral.
  - Fever >39°C persisting >48 h – consider bacterial superinfection.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Routine: no specific follow-up unless complications occur. Advise GP if symptoms not improving by 1 week.
  - Audiological follow-up if any hearing symptoms.
  - Fertility counselling if bilateral orchitis with persistent testicular atrophy: refer to andrology clinic.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** (especially with neurological deterioration).
  - **GCS <15** or any new focal neurology → ?encephalitis; need urgent CT + LP.
  - **Seizures** → treat as status; call senior anaesthetist/neurologist.
  - **Severe scrotal pain with absent cremasteric reflex** → urgent urology referral and Doppler ultrasound (within 1 hour).
  - **Airway compromise** (stridor, drooling, inability to swallow secretions) → call anaesthetics, prepare for difficult airway.
  - **Suspected intracranial hypertension** (papilloedema, Cushing’s reflex) → senior review, consider CT and neurosurgery referral.
